design_philosophy.core_loop: Complete stages by defeating obstacles and enemies, acquire special weapons from Robot Master bosses, use weapons strategically to progress through subsequent stages
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Pattern recognition and execution precision against escalating enemy and boss difficulty; dual-character asymmetrical mechanics create divergent playstyle tensions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Master a signature robot with unique abilities (charged shots vs. rapid-fire multi-directional fire) to defeat increasingly powerful adversaries and prove superiority
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Intentionally targets younger/less-advanced players despite series transition to newer platforms; enforces single-character commitment per playthrough, creating distinct mechanical philosophies between Mega Man (charge/precision) and Bass (speed/multi-direction); steep difficulty despite accessibility intent signals designers valued challenge integrity over lowered skill floors

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection (Mega Man vs Bass) creates branching difficulty profiles; Mega Man's charge mechanic and slide-fire offer deliberate positioning; Bass's rapid multi-directional fire and double-jump/dash provide mobility advantages but lock firing in place on ground. Critics noted difficulty was steep despite accessibility targeting

progression.upgrade_system: Acquired special weapons from defeated Robot Masters; Bass has equippable upgrades (wall-piercing shot capability mentioned)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Robot Master defeats sequentially unlock stage access and weapon acquisition
replayability_hooks: Two distinct character playstyles force replay for alternative mechanical experience
Character-specific narrative variations (story differs by protagonist choice)
Asymmetrical weapons and abilities between Mega Man and Bass create separate optimal strategies per stage

world_building_techniques: Stage-based world progression (Robot Museum, King's Castle, Dr. Wily's Laboratory)
Character interactions revealing motivations (Mega Man's duty to humans, Bass's rivalry drive, King's utopian robot ideology, Proto Man's free will philosophy)
Dual-narrative structure where character choice alters story perspective and encounters
Post-game character letter epilogue (King's redemption arc closure)

boss_design.philosophy: Eight Robot Masters serve as stage climaxes with signature weapons; King functions as mid-final-boss with shield phase requiring Proto Man's intervention; Dr. Wily returns as final boss. Design philosophy emphasizes weapon acquisition as mechanic driver and pattern-based combat
notable_bosses: Eight Robot Masters under King's allegiance (weapon-granting)
King (mid-boss with shield phase, later empowered by Wily's brainwashing)
Dr. Wily (final boss, appears on monitor before final confrontation)

faction_indicators: Mega Man: duty to humans, protector role, commanded by Dr. Light
Bass: rivalry-driven, desires to prove strength against Mega Man, initially deceived by Wily
King: utopian robot supremacist, seeks dominion over humans, created by Wily as test
Proto Man: independent free will, refuses King's offer, destroys Wily's plans, philosophical counterpoint to mechanistic obedience
danger_signals: King's theft of creator blueprints signals threat to established order
Wily's deception of Bass and creation of King as 'test' reveals manipulative control
King's brainwashing enhancement suggests loss of autonomy and escalating power
Robot supremacy ideology explicit in King's dialogue challenging why robots serve humans
environmental_storytelling: Robot Museum as contested space where rival factions converge
King's Castle infiltration represents enemy stronghold progression
Dr. Wily's Laboratory as recurring villain headquarters reinforcing series continuity
Self-destruct sequence and castle collapse emphasize destructive resolution
